commentary:
**وَٱلضُّحَىٰ**

*By the bright morning sunlight*

Allah ﷻ opens this Surah with a powerful oath, swearing by the time of Ad-Duha - the bright morning sunlight. This refers to that blessed time around 9 or 9:30 AM when the sun's light is brilliant yet not scorching. It's a moment when you can enjoy the sun's brightness without being burned by its heat, when light is coupled with coolness and comfort.

This Surah expresses a deeply intimate relationship between Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ. It stands as an expression of the profound love Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ share with each other. While the previous Surah highlighted the highest level of piety a human can reach, embodied in the ranks of the Prophets, the Siddiqeen (those who accept truth without hesitation), the Shuhada (those who bear witness), and the Saliheen (the righteous), this Surah now addresses that supreme station of Prophethood itself.

translation: And [by] the night when it covers with darkness
commentary:
**وَٱلَّيْلِ إِذَا سَجَىٰ**

*And by the night as it becomes still*

The word سَجَىٰ carries profound meaning. It's used to describe the ocean when its waves become completely still, or when a sheep's wool lies flat and calm. When applied to the night, it refers to that time when everything becomes utterly silent - not even a single breeze stirs, not a leaf moves. It's a quietness that resembles death itself.

These two images - the soothing morning light and the still, lifeless night - represent two distinct experiences. Some scholars have understood the morning light as a beautiful way of describing revelation itself. Revelation is light, but unlike harsh light that burns, it soothes and brings comfort. The long night that had passed represents the darkness of misguidance that prevailed before the Quran's light began to descend.

Others have interpreted the night as referring to those blessed times when the Prophet ﷺ would recite the Quran in his home during the late hours. In those days, homes were not soundproof, so when he would recite and pray, his neighbors could hear it. His immediate neighbor was actually Abu Lahab, and this historical context adds another layer to understanding these ayahs.

However, there's a deeper dimension to these opening oaths. They reflect two lived experiences of the Prophet ﷺ - the coming of revelation that brought him joy and fulfillment, and then the gap in revelation that left him in darkness, wondering if it would ever return. The morning represents that beautiful experience of receiving Allah's ﷻ words, while the still night captures the emptiness and anxiety he felt when revelation stopped coming.

To truly understand what follows, we must appreciate what this gap meant for the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. He had no way of knowing that Allah ﷻ would grant him 23 years of revelation. At that moment, with only a handful of revelations received and then nothing - days or even weeks passing with no word from Allah ﷻ - he didn't know if revelation would ever return. Perhaps he had done something wrong. Perhaps he was no longer worthy. These thoughts weighed heavily on his heart.

Making matters worse, the woman who lived nearby - Um Jamil, wife of Abu Lahab - began mocking him. When she noticed the nights had gone quiet, no longer filled with the Prophet's ﷺ recitation, she said with contempt, "Your devil has left you. I hope he never comes back."

It was in this moment of deep uncertainty and hurt that Allah ﷻ revealed these verses of comfort and reassurance.

translation: Your Lord has not taken leave of you, [O Muhammad], nor has He detested [you]
commentary:
**مَا وَدَّعَكَ رَبُّكَ وَمَا قَلَىٰ**

*Your Master has not abandoned you, nor is He displeased*

The choice of the word مَا here is significant. In Arabic, there are multiple ways to express negation, but ma is used specifically to correct someone - to tell them, "No, you're wrong." Allah ﷻ is correcting the wrong notion that may have been forming in the Prophet's ﷺ heart. The sadness and fear that perhaps Allah ﷻ was unhappy with him is gently but firmly addressed.

The word وَدَّعَ means to say farewell, to leave something behind and never return to it, like sealing off a grave and never going back. It's used for abandonment in the deepest sense. Allah ﷻ is telling His beloved Messenger ﷺ: "I have not abandoned you. I have not left you alone."

Then comes قَلَىٰ - a word that means to be disgusted with something after using it, like detergent you scrub your clothes with and then wash away. It carries the meaning of finding something worthless and dismissing it. Allah ﷻ says: "I am not upset with you. I have not found you worthless. I am not disappointed in you."

This ayah speaks not only to the Prophet ﷺ but to every believer who has experienced those beautiful moments of closeness to Allah ﷻ - times when tears flow easily in prayer, when your heart feels connected to your Creator, when you don't want to lift your head from sajdah. But then those moments pass, and you wander far from that spiritual state. You can barely remember what that closeness felt like. The darkness feels overwhelming, and you think, "I've made too many mistakes. I can never get back there."

To all of us, Allah ﷻ gives hope through these words to His Messenger ﷺ. Just as the Messenger ﷺ had to experience this gap - partly so he could relate to our experience and give us hope - we too must understand that distance from Allah ﷻ doesn't mean He has abandoned us. People around us might make us feel worthless, and we might start projecting that onto Allah's ﷻ view of us. But this is a grave mistake that this ayah corrects.

translation: And the Hereafter is better for you than the first [life]
commentary:
**وَلَلْـَٔاخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لَّكَ مِنَ ٱلْأُولَىٰ**

*And surely the end is better for you than the beginning*

The لَ at the beginning emphasizes the oath - "I swear to you." What is at the very end will be better for you than what came at the very beginning. This promise operates on multiple levels.

Some have understood this as referring to Jannah itself - what awaits the Prophet ﷺ in the Hereafter cannot compare to anything in this world. On the Day of Judgment, Allah ﷻ will give him special honors: the Maqam Mahmood (the Praised Station), the flag of Hamd (praise), and permission to make intercession when no one else will speak except by Rahman's permission.

But there's another compelling interpretation rooted in the immediate context. Right now, the Prophet ﷺ thinks the gap in revelation might be the end. But Allah ﷻ is telling him: "This isn't the end. By the time you reach the conclusion of your mission, you'll see how much better things have become."

The Prophet ﷺ didn't leave behind buildings, monuments, or vast treasures. When great kings and pharaohs died, they left pyramids and statues. But the Prophet ﷺ was born in a desert, lived in a desert, and left it a desert. What did he leave behind? Human beings whose hearts were filled with the word of Allah ﷻ. That is a greater legacy than any structure. Those hearts changed the entire map of the planet.

The Prophet ﷺ is compared to a farmer who, when the crop grows tall and mature, is filled with happiness at what he has cultivated. The maturity of the Sahaba (RA), their growth and development, would be his source of joy. This is why one day you and I can stand and say "La ilaha illa Allah, Muhammad ar-Rasul Allah."

The mission won't just be a phase that ends. Allah ﷻ will see it through to completion. This is the promise of this ayah.

translation: And your Lord is going to give you, and you will be satisfied
commentary:
**وَلَسَوْفَ يُعْطِيكَ رَبُّكَ فَتَرْضَىٰ**

*And your Master will certainly give to you, and you will be pleased*

The سَوْفَ indicates "very soon" - a promise of imminent fulfillment. Your Rabb will grant you, and then you will be pleased.

Notice what Allah ﷻ doesn't specify: what exactly will He give? This deliberate ambiguity leaves the door wide open to endless possibilities. Whatever you can imagine, Allah ﷻ will give - followers, spiritual contentment, Jannah, the confirmation that the mission is complete, the Fountain of Kawthar, the completion of the Quran's revelation. The list goes on because Allah ﷻ chose not to limit it.

This open-ended promise itself brings joy. Think of a child who's told, "I'm going to give you a present, and you're going to be really happy." The happiness begins immediately - "What is it? What is it?" The anticipation itself is a gift.

Consider a poor man living in a hut who suddenly receives word that his wealthy uncle has died and left him everything. For the 30 days before he can claim his inheritance, he's still living in that hut, still eating simple food, but he's the happiest man alive. Why? Because the guarantee of what's coming changes everything about how he experiences the present.

This is what Allah ﷻ gave His Messenger ﷺ - a guarantee that created unshakable optimism. No matter how bad things became, no matter who was tortured or killed, no matter how many conspiracies arose to harm him, the Prophet ﷺ held firm to this promise. Things will get better. Allah ﷻ will see this through.

This is like Musa (AS) standing at the edge of the sea with Pharaoh's army approaching. The people cried, "We're going to die - either by drowning or by the sword!" But Musa (AS) said with absolute certainty, "كَلَّا ۖ إِنَّ مَعِيَ رَبِّي سَيَهْدِينِ - No! My Master is with me. He will guide me." And the sea opened before him.

This is the kind of optimism and trust in Allah's ﷻ plan that this ayah instills.

translation: Did He not find you an orphan and give [you] refuge
commentary:
**أَلَمْ يَجِدْكَ يَتِيمًا فَـَٔاوَىٰ**

*Did He not find you as an orphan and give you shelter?*

Now begins the evidence, the proof of why the Prophet ﷺ should never doubt Allah's ﷻ care. These next three ayahs present three cases where Allah ﷻ already demonstrated His unwavering support.

The word آوَىٰ means to give refuge, security, shelter - to wrap someone up safely where they cannot be harmed. Imagine someone running from danger, knocking on your door desperately, and you take them in, close the door, and make them feel completely secure. That's awa.

The Prophet ﷺ lost his parents at an extremely young age. Then his grandfather, who had become his source of comfort, passed away when he was still a child. Then he was taken in by his uncle Abu Talib, who later died during the early years of his Prophethood. He went from one caretaker to another.

But Allah ﷻ says: all of those transitions, all of those stops in your life - that was Me providing for you. I gave you shelter. I healed the wound of losing your parents. I made sure you were taken care of.

The people of pre-Islamic Arabia used to treat orphans terribly. Children would be left homeless, wandering the streets, begging, annoying people who would shoo them away like stray animals. This was the common culture.

When Allah ﷻ reminds the Prophet ﷺ of his orphanhood and how He provided for him, it's not just about the past. It's preparation for what comes later in the Surah. Since you know what it's like to be vulnerable, since you know what it's like to need protection, now you must become that source of security for others.

translation: And He found you lost and guided [you]
commentary:
**وَوَجَدَكَ ضَآلًّا فَهَدَىٰ**

*And He found you lost and guided you*

The word ضَالّ typically means "misguided" or "lost," but we must be extremely careful with this word when discussing the Prophet ﷺ. He was never misguided in the sense of being on the wrong path morally or spiritually.

The word dall can mean someone who is at a fork in the road, not knowing which path to take - standing still, unable to move forward because they don't know the way. This is the meaning that applies here. The Prophet ﷺ was at a loss for where to go, what to do.

Before revelation came, the Prophet ﷺ was living a comfortable life. He was married to Khadijah (RA), an extremely supportive wife. Business was stable. But he became increasingly troubled by what he saw in Makkan society - the oppression, the injustice, baby girls being buried alive, idol worship, fornication, and adultery. All of this ate away at him.

He would ask Khadijah (RA) for permission to spend time alone contemplating, and she supported him in this. He would go to the cave of Hira and just think deeply about these problems. How do you solve this? How do you remove this evil? Where is the guidance?

He was at a standstill, desperately seeking answers but not knowing where to turn. That is when Allah ﷻ sent him the greatest guidance of all time through the Angel Jibreel (AS).

Allah ﷻ is making the case: "You were an orphan, and I secured you. You were lost and searching, and I guided you."

translation: And He found you poor and made [you] self-sufficient
commentary:
**وَوَجَدَكَ عَآئِلًا فَأَغْنَىٰ**

*And He found you in need and made you independent*

The word عَائِل means someone in desperate need, someone bankrupt, someone at a complete loss. This applies in two ways.

First, there was a bankruptcy of the heart. The Prophet ﷺ felt a deep emptiness seeing all the evil around him, not knowing how to bring contentment to his heart in the face of such corruption.

Second, there was financial bankruptcy. Abu Talib, his uncle and protector, was actually the poorest of the uncles. While Abu Lahab and others were wealthy, Abu Talib struggled financially. Later in life, it was actually the Prophet ﷺ who supported Abu Talib's family, not the other way around. But tribally, Abu Talib provided him protection.

Allah ﷻ provided the Prophet ﷺ with financial security through his marriage to Khadijah (RA), and spiritual security through revelation.

These three ayahs together paint a picture: Allah ﷻ manufactured the Prophet's ﷺ life in a way that would free him up to become a messenger. As a child, he would not have survived unless Allah ﷻ made all the necessary means available. As he grew older, if he had been constantly grinding away at business just to make ends meet, he would not have had the time to contemplate and seek answers as he needed to.

Allah ﷻ created a secure environment in his life so that he could free himself - not to relax and chill, but to seek the truth and goodness for humanity.

When you and I are working constantly or studying all the time, when do we have time to truly think and contemplate? We're just in the grind - wake up, do the thing, collapse, repeat. We become zombies. The Prophet ﷺ needed that space, cut off from the constant tussles of life, to be ready to receive Allah's ﷻ guidance.

This is Allah's ﷻ way of telling His Messenger ﷺ: "Look, I didn't leave you. I haven't abandoned you. I've actually fulfilled this for you three times already. When you were an orphan, were you not fine? Did I not take care of you? Didn't things turn out better - wasn't the end better than the beginning at that time? When you were searching for guidance and desperate, didn't I give you guidance? Wasn't that ending better? When you were bankrupt, didn't I take care of you this third time? So don't think I'll leave you now. Your life itself is proof that I have never left you."

This is the lesson for all of us. When we face desperate times, we must look back at the moments when Allah ﷻ was there for us. Remind yourself - that's the same Rabb you have now. He's still there. You might not be connecting to Him, but that doesn't mean He's not connecting to you.

translation: So as for the orphan, do not oppress [him]
commentary:
**فَأَمَّا ٱلْيَتِيمَ فَلَا تَقْهَرْ**

*Then as for the orphan, do not oppress*

The فَ here indicates "therefore" - because Allah ﷻ has done all this for you, here's what you must do.

Notice the special emphasis on the orphan by bringing the word forward in the sentence structure. This highlights and draws attention to the orphan in a way that wouldn't otherwise be emphasized.

This is the second time the word "orphan" appears in this Surah. The first time was about the Prophet ﷺ himself. Since you were an orphan and you know what it's like, and right now you're going through pain wondering when revelation will return to give you comfort - Allah ﷻ commands His Messenger ﷺ: focus your energy on those who need your help. Focus on the orphan, and in doing so, you will find contentment.

There's a beautiful indication in this ayah that every time we take care of an orphan, we should see in them a glimpse of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ himself. That orphan shares something profound with Rasul Allah ﷺ. Our love for the Prophet ﷺ should naturally flow into love and care for orphans. These two things are deeply interconnected.

The key word here is تَقْهَر. This comes from قَهْر, which means to overpower, dominate, intimidate, or overwhelm someone. It's when you handle something delicate roughly, when you impose yourself on someone leaving them no choice, when you make someone feel paralyzed and submissive.

You can do qahr with physical force, but you can also do it with words. You can guilt-trip someone, shame them, accuse them, and beat them down emotionally without ever raising a hand. You can even do qahr with just a look - a glance that makes someone melt in their seat, that strips away their dignity.

Scholars say that even just a dismissive gesture toward an orphan can be qahr. Don't make the orphan feel intimidated. Don't talk to them in a humiliating way. Don't rip their dignity away from them.

This word qahr was used by Pharaoh when he said about the Israelites, "إِنَّا فَوْقَهُمْ قَاهِرُونَ - We will make them feel worthless." Allah ﷻ tells His Messenger ﷺ: Never make an orphan feel worthless.

Remember, this echoes something the Prophet ﷺ himself was just told: "مَا قَلَىٰ - He has not found you worthless." Now Allah ﷻ says: Never let an orphan feel worthless.

But there's more to this than just avoiding harm. The command is not just "don't give them food and run off" or "here's a dollar, now get lost." That doesn't solve the problem. The word used earlier was آوَىٰ - shelter, security, refuge. Allah ﷻ didn't just give the Prophet ﷺ one meal at a time; He gave him comprehensive care.

So when Allah ﷻ says "don't oppress the orphan," He's essentially saying: Give them shelter like I gave you shelter. Provide them with institutions. Make sure they have homes, education, and empowerment. Don't let them remain in a state of despair.

translation: And as for the petitioner, do not repel [him]
commentary:
**وَأَمَّا ٱلسَّآئِلَ فَلَا تَنْهَرْ**

*And as for the one who asks, do not repel*

The word تَنْهَر comes from نَهْر, which means a river. When a river flows with force, it overwhelms everything in its path. When you scold someone harshly, raising your voice and shutting them down so completely that they cannot respond - you've overrun them like a river.

The سَائِل - the one who asks - can refer to two types of people.

First, it can mean a beggar, someone in financial need, someone bankrupt. This connects directly back to the earlier ayah about the Prophet ﷺ himself being عَائِل (in need), and Allah ﷻ making him غَنِي (independent).

Second, it can mean a questioner - someone seeking knowledge, someone who is lost and trying to find guidance. This connects to the earlier mention of the Prophet ﷺ being ضَالّ (searching for direction), and Allah ﷻ guiding him.

So this one word covers both the spiritually lost who come with questions and the materially needy who come asking for help.

Don't make them feel bad because they have questions. Don't make them feel stupid for asking. Don't yell at them: "What kind of question is that? Don't you have something better to ask? You're wasting my time. My talk was about this and your question is about that."

When we attend lectures and conferences, we collect written questions from the audience afterward. I can guarantee you that 90% of those questions have nothing to do with the talk that was just given. The questions reflect what's actually on people's minds: "Is nail polish haram? Can I have a pet rabbit?" All kinds of questions.

And the speaker might sit there thinking, "I only want to take the relevant questions." But here's the problem: when we're teaching or speaking, we need to realize that we didn't do the people a favor by coming there. They honored us by listening. We are at the service of the people. Their questions should be our priority.

If this is what's on their minds, then we need to help them with what's on their minds - not tell them, "Forget about what you think is important. Let me tell you what's important for you." That's not how it's supposed to work.

We've unfortunately created a culture where people feel they must serve the celebrity speaker. But actually, the teacher, the da'i, is a servant to the people. You're supposed to be there to answer their questions. It's a public service, not a public status position.

This principle was given to the Prophet ﷺ himself, who would become the greatest and most awe-inspiring figure in human history. You would think people would be nervous about asking him questions, worried about asking the "right" thing. But notice something remarkable: the Sahaba (RA) used to come and ask him anything. They asked all kinds of things with no hesitation.

Once someone asked, "Every one of us has a devil assigned to us. Do you have one too?" Can you imagine actually asking that? And the Prophet ﷺ didn't say, "Excuse me? How dare you? I'm the Messenger of Allah!" He simply answered, "Yes, but mine has become Muslim."

Angels asked Allah ﷻ questions: "Are You really going to put humans on earth who will spill blood?" If the angels can ask Allah ﷻ, then surely you can ask anyone. The Messenger ﷺ is being instructed: You're in this position now - you better not turn somebody away who comes with a question.

I provided you shelter. I provided you guidance. I provided you with wealth. Now you become a source of all three for others. This is وَأَمَّا ٱلسَّآئِلَ فَلَا تَنْهَرْ.

translation: But as for the favor of your Lord, report [it]
commentary:
**وَأَمَّا بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ فَحَدِّثْ**

*And as for the favor of your Master, speak about it*

This final command has generated much scholarly discussion because it seems simple on the surface but carries profound implications.

If Allah ﷻ is saying "talk about the favors He's given you," does that mean we should go around announcing, "Alhamdulillah, Allah gave me so much knowledge. Alhamdulillah, Allah gave me a beautiful house. Alhamdulillah, Allah gave me..." Wouldn't that sound like showing off? Wouldn't that make others feel less blessed? Wouldn't it create tension between those who have and those who don't?

Some Sahaba (RA) were praised by others for their good qualities. When asked, "Why don't you tell us about yourself? What good qualities do you have?" they would respond, "Allah ﷻ has forbidden us from self-praise and self-righteousness." But isn't Allah ﷻ commanding us here to talk about His favors?

Others reconciled this by saying the favors we should talk about are acts of worship. "Allah blessed me with completing half the Quran yesterday" or "Allah blessed me with praying tahajjud." But even this interpretation was challenged. Some scholars argued that your good deeds are not what Allah ﷻ is referring to when He says "My favor" - those are your actions, not purely His gift.

The key to understanding this ayah lies in looking back at what the Surah itself identifies as Allah's ﷻ favors. What are the three things Allah ﷻ explicitly mentioned as His gifts to the Prophet ﷺ?

1. آوَىٰ - He gave you shelter when you were an orphan
2. هَدَىٰ - He guided you when you were lost  
3. أَغْنَىٰ - He made you independent when you were in need

These weren't times when the Prophet ﷺ was doing good deeds. These were times when he was in crisis and Allah ﷻ came to his aid. That is Allah's ﷻ favor in this Surah.

So when you talk about Allah's ﷻ favor, talk about the times you were in crisis and Allah ﷻ rescued you. This benefits others immensely. When someone hears, "I was in desperate need and Allah provided for me," they think, "If He did that for them, He can do it for me too." It gives hope.

How many stories have you heard of people who were lost and Allah ﷻ guided them to Islam? How many testimonies have you encountered of people who were sick and Allah ﷻ gave them healing? How many accounts of people going bankrupt and Allah ﷻ providing for them? These stories inspire others. This is بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ فَحَدِّثْ.

This is not about boasting "I'm such an awesome person." We're not understanding it correctly if we go in that direction.

## The Coherent Structure of the Surah

This Surah follows a progressive structure, building its argument step by step.

**Stage One: Removing Sadness and Giving Hope**

The opening message is clear: just as the coming of revelation is necessary, gaps in revelation are also necessary. Just as day and night are both essential, the presence and absence of revelation both serve a purpose. Allah ﷻ has not abandoned you. He's not upset. The end will be better than the beginning. You're going to be very happy. Soon, very soon, your Master will give to you and you will be pleased.

This removes sadness and replaces it with hope and optimism.

**Stage Two: Evidence from the Past**

Then Allah ﷻ says: You already have enough reasons to be optimistic even if I hadn't told you this. Just reflect on your own past. You were an orphan - I gave you shelter. You were lost - I guided you. You were in need - I made you independent.

If a human being has these three things in life - Allah's ﷻ protection (آوَىٰ), Allah's ﷻ guidance (هَدَىٰ), and independence from desperate need of others (أَغْنَىٰ) - what greater blessing could you ask for? With these three gifts, you have a good life. These three are not just something Allah ﷻ gave the Prophet ﷺ; they represent the ultimate favor any human being can receive.

This is enough. This is complete. This demonstrates that Allah ﷻ has been taking care of you all along.

**Stage Three: Extending Hope to Others**

Now that you realize Allah ﷻ has taken care of you and will continue to take care of you, it's time to shift your attention away from yourself and toward those who need this hope too. You were hopeless and I gave you hope - now become a source of hope for others.

Who do you start with? The orphan - because you were an orphan (يَتِيم).

Who do you go to next? The one who asks (سَائِل) - because you were someone seeking guidance (ضَالّ) and you were someone in need (عَائِل).

Notice the perfect correspondence: the three gifts Allah ﷻ gave are now the three responsibilities He assigns.

**The Final Command: Share Your Story**

The last ayah ties everything together. When you go take care of an orphan, remind them that you were once an orphan too. It will be okay. When someone comes to you lost with questions, let them know there was a time you were searching too. It will be okay. When someone is in bankruptcy and desperate need, don't just give them material help - give them hope by reminding them of Allah's ﷻ favor on you.

This is how we become sources of inspiration, not just givers of charity. It's not enough to simply hand something to an orphan. The Surah says فَلَا تَقْهَرْ - don't oppress them, meaning honor them, give them shelter, don't humiliate them, and give them hope by talking about what Allah ﷻ has done for you.

The ultimate favor of Allah ﷻ on the Prophet ﷺ is contained in the promise "سَوْفَ يُعْطِيكَ رَبُّكَ فَتَرْضَىٰ - Your Lord will give to you and you will be pleased." That guarantee itself is the greatest gift. For us, by extension, the greatest favor is the Quran - the complete, preserved word of Allah ﷻ.

Our ultimate expression of gratitude is talking about the word of Allah ﷻ, but not in isolation. We talk about it as we take care of those in desperate need, as we care for the orphan, as we help the one who asks. Through this, Allah ﷻ softens our hearts toward the Quran and increases our love for His final Messenger ﷺ.

May Allah ﷻ grant us the ability to recognize His favors upon us, to never despair in His mercy, and to become sources of hope and support for those around us, just as He commanded His beloved Messenger ﷺ.
